title: Hilton Worldwide Holdings Inc. (HLT) — Intrinsic Value & Prospect Score
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Hilton Worldwide (HLT) compiled by Gemini 3.1. Economic Prospect Score: 82/100 — Strong Prospect.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: An 8.0% growth rate is aggressive but achievable, driven by Hilton's massive development pipeline and net unit growth (NUG). Because the asset-light model requires minimal capital expenditures, top-line fee revenue growth converts powerfully into free cash flow expansion.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 9.0% discount rate reflects the cyclicality of the hospitality industry and Hilton's beta (1.12), balanced by the stability and predictability of its recurring franchise fee revenue streams.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 3.0% terminal growth rate sits at the higher end of conservative estimates, reflecting Hilton's powerful global brand equity and ability to capture an expanding middle class globally, slightly outpacing base U.S. inflation.

thesisParagraphs: Hilton Worldwide operates one of the most attractive business models in public markets. By pivoting almost entirely to an asset-light franchisor, it offloads the capital intensity and economic sensitivity of real estate ownership onto developers. Instead, Hilton acts as a toll collector, taking a percentage of top-line revenue from every branded hotel in exchange for access to its reservation system, massive loyalty program, and brand equity.

While the underlying business is exceptional, the current valuation reflects overwhelming market optimism. The stock trades at a significant premium to its calculated intrinsic value, implying that the market is pricing in flawless execution, aggressive unit growth, and zero macroeconomic turbulence over the next decade. While Hilton is a premier compounder, buying at these elevated multiples offers no margin of safety for the inherent cyclicality of global travel.
verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: If Hilton's business model is so good, why is it considered overvalued?
faq.answer: Intrinsic value calculations are based on future cash flows, not just business quality. The market has bid up Hilton's price so high that it requires perfection (no recessions, uninterrupted global travel growth) to justify the current valuation. There is no margin of safety built into the current price.

faq.question: What is an 'asset-light' business model?
faq.answer: It means Hilton generally doesn't own the physical hotel buildings. They franchise their brand and manage the reservation systems. This requires very little capital to maintain, allowing them to turn a massive percentage of their revenue into pure profit (free cash flow).

faq.question: How did Gemini calculate the intrinsic value of $160.33?
faq.answer: The value was calculated using a 10-year Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model. It takes current free cash flow, projects it out 5 years at an 8% growth rate, applies a 3% terminal rate, and discounts it all back to the present using a 9% cost of capital. The resulting sum is divided by total outstanding shares.

prospectVerdictDetail: Hilton operates a highly attractive, asset-light business model that generates substantial free cash flow and insulates the company from the capital intensity of hotel ownership. With a dominant global brand presence and an expanding loyalty program (Hilton Honors), it possesses a wide and durable economic moat. Robust unit growth and strong pricing power continue to drive compounding returns, making it a premier compounder in the hospitality sector.

competitiveMomentum.title: Competitive Momentum
competitiveMomentum.summary: Hilton demonstrates exceptional competitive momentum. By executing an asset-light franchising model, it rapidly scales its global footprint while consistently increasing RevPAR (Revenue Per Available Room).

factors.name: Revenue Growth vs. Peers
factors.rationale: Hilton consistently posts strong revenue growth, largely driven by net unit growth (adding new hotels to its system) and robust fee revenues that outpace traditional asset-heavy hospitality peers.

factors.name: Market Share Trajectory
factors.rationale: The company continues to aggressively capture market share, particularly in the midscale and upper-midscale segments. It boasts one of the largest development pipelines in the industry, ensuring future share gains.

factors.name: Pricing Power
factors.rationale: Hilton exhibits strong pricing power, evidenced by sustained increases in Average Daily Rate (ADR) across its portfolio. Its diverse brand architecture allows it to capture demand across various price points.

factors.name: Product Velocity
factors.rationale: The company frequently innovates its brand portfolio, launching new flags (like Spark and LivSmart Studios) targeted at specific, underserved consumer and developer niches to accelerate development.

moatDurability.title: Moat Durability
moatDurability.summary: Hilton's economic moat is wide and formidable, built upon the intangible asset of its brand and the powerful network effects generated by the massive Hilton Honors loyalty program.

factors.name: Switching Costs
factors.rationale: For hotel owners, switching franchises involves significant capital outlays (rebranding, new systems) and potential loss of revenue. For consumers, the Hilton Honors program creates sticky loyalty through tiered benefits and points accumulation.

factors.name: Network Effects
factors.rationale: A classic two-sided network effect: more hotels attract more Hilton Honors members, while a larger member base makes the Hilton flag more attractive to real estate developers seeking to maximize occupancy.

factors.name: Regulatory & IP Position
factors.rationale: Hilton holds immense brand equity. Regulatory risks are generally localized to specific markets (e.g., labor laws, zoning), which are largely the responsibility of the franchisee, not Hilton corporate.

factors.name: Capital Intensity Advantage
factors.rationale: As an asset-light franchisor and manager, Hilton owns very few physical hotels. This results in incredibly low capital expenditures, allowing the vast majority of operating cash flow to convert directly into free cash flow.

sentimentCatalyst.title: Sentiment & Catalysts
sentimentCatalyst.summary: Market sentiment is strongly positive, recognizing Hilton's compounding free cash flow. The primary risk is macroeconomic, as the stock is heavily exposed to travel demand and consumer discretionary spending.

factors.name: Earnings Estimate Revisions
factors.rationale: Analysts have generally maintained or slightly raised estimates, reflecting confidence in Hilton's pipeline execution and resilient leisure demand, despite lingering concerns over a broader economic slowdown.

factors.name: News & Narrative Sentiment
factors.rationale: The narrative remains favorable, focusing on the strength of the asset-light model. However, elevated valuations leave the stock vulnerable to any negative headlines regarding consumer spending or travel trends.

factors.name: Management & Capital Allocation
factors.rationale: Under Chris Nassetta, management execution has been superb. The company employs its massive free cash flow almost entirely toward shareholder returns, executing consistent, large-scale share repurchase programs.

prospectKeyRisks: A severe macroeconomic recession that structurally impairs business travel budgets and consumer discretionary spending on leisure.
Rising interest rates and tightening credit markets that could slow the pace of new hotel construction by developers, impacting Hilton's unit growth pipeline.
Intense competition from other major global franchisors (like Marriott) and alternative lodging platforms (like Airbnb) fighting for market share.
prospectKeyCatalysts: Accelerating international expansion, particularly in the Asia-Pacific region, driving a massive increase in net unit growth.
Continued robust share repurchase programs, leveraging the asset-light model's free cash flow to continually reduce share count and boost EPS.
Successful scaling of newly launched premium economy and extended-stay brands, capturing new demographics of both consumers and developers.
prospectMethodology: Consensus Analysis — Economic Prospect Score averaging independent evaluations from Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1. Gemini scored HLT at 82/100 and Opus at 82/100. Each factor score is the arithmetic mean of both models. Three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30).
